James begins this chapter by tracing our conflicts back to their true source: the warfare raging inside us. We fight others because we are enslaved to desires we cannot satisfy. The devastating honesty here is that we often know exactly what we lack, we even ask God for it—but we ask with poisoned motives, wanting to devour what we receive on our own pleasure. This is not passive temptation; this is active betrayal. To be "a friend of the world" while claiming allegiance to God is spiritual adultery. We cannot serve both. God's Spirit does not dwell in us passively; He yearns intensely for our undivided hearts. He grieves when we treat Him like one option among many.

Yet James does not leave us abandoned to our duplicity. In the middle of this indictment stands a promise that transforms everything: God gives more grace. The pathway out is radically simple but demands total reorientation. Submit to God. Resist the devil. Draw near to God, and He will draw near to you. But notice what comes before the comfort—we must cleanse our hands and purify our hearts; we must grieve our sin genuinely, not gloss over it with false joy. Humility is not merely an attitude; it is the posture of those who have stopped pretending and started returning to God.

James then turns to the sins we commit against one another, exposing how judgment of our brothers is actually judgment against God's law itself. We assume an authority we do not possess. There is one Lawgiver, one Judge, and it is not us. The relief of this should liberate us—we are not responsible for condemning others, and we are not permitted to try. Finally, James confronts our arrogance about the future. We speak of our plans with certainty, as if tomorrow is guaranteed, as if we are the masters of our own days. But life is a mist. Our existence hangs on the will of God. Every claim we make about what we will do should be qualified by submission to His sovereignty. If we know what is right and refuse to do it, we have already sinned. Knowledge without obedience is not faith; it is rebellion.
